title: Elderberry
subtitle: Sambucus nigra, the hedgerow's autumn berry
excerpt: Single-herb profile. The black-purple berries of the European elder, used in syrups and decoctions through the cold season. Always cooked, never raw.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), elder entry, comprehensive treatment of every part of the tree, including the cyanogenic-glycoside warning on raw berries. Nicholas Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), the seventeenth-century English record. EMA HMPC monograph on Sambuci fructus (2014), modern conservative summary and dosing. Cochrane and PubMed open-access reviews of elderberry-syrup clinical trials (2019, 2021), the evidence base for the cold-season use, conservatively presented.

content.text: Elderberry is the hedgerow's autumn berry. The kitchen takes it in syrups and warm simmered drinks through the cold season. Deep-purple berries hang in flat-topped clusters in late August through September across the British Isles and continental Europe. They grow on the same tree as the creamy May flowers (elderflower) of spring cordial. The berries are the herbal kitchen's cold-and-flu staple. Old herbals called the elder tree "the medicine chest of the country people".

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Always cook before eating. Raw elderberries hold cyanogenic glycosides, which release small amounts of cyanide when broken down. Cooking destroys them. Every traditional preparation simmers the berries for at least 30 minutes. No raw-elderberry preparation is in this library.

Plant ID: if you forage rather than buy dried berries, positive ID is on you. Common elder has black-purple berries in flat-topped clusters. Dwarf elder has similar berries but is toxic to take internally. Its berries also point upward, not down. Mountain ash, deadly nightshade, and pokeweed have all been confused with elderberry by beginners. If you cannot ID the plant, use cultivated dried berries from a reputable supplier.

Autoimmune conditions: elderberry shifts the immune response. Be careful with regular use if you have lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, MS, or an autoimmune thyroid. Talk to a herbalist first.

Diabetes and heart: long daily strong doses can lower blood sugar a little, and act as a mild diuretic. If you take diabetes drugs, blood-pressure drugs, or diuretics, mention elderberry-syrup use to your prescriber when taking it daily for more than a couple of weeks.

Pregnancy: the cooked-berry syrup at standard cold-season doses (1 to 2 teaspoons up to three times a day) is widely tolerated. Strong tinctures are best discussed with a herbalist or midwife.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

content.text: Botanical

Sambucus nigra is a small tree or large shrub of the Adoxaceae family, common in hedgerows, woodland edges, and waste ground across temperate Europe. It reaches 3-5 metres at maturity, with pinnate leaves of 5-7 serrated leaflets and a deeply furrowed grey bark on older specimens.

Flowering: May to early June in southern Britain, late May to mid-June further north. The creamy white flowers grow in flat-topped umbel clusters 10 to 25 cm across. A heady scent. The kitchen meets that flower as elderflower cordial. The flower is a separate library entry, used quite differently from the berry.

Fruiting: August to September in southern Britain, September to early October further north. The berries appear in the same flat-topped cluster pattern, ripening from green to red to deep purple-black. Ripe clusters hang heavy with the weight; unripe red-stage berries should be left to mature. Birds are fast competitors, clusters left on the tree past the deep-purple stage are usually stripped within a day.

Look-alikes worth knowing. Dwarf elder is toxic. It looks similar but dies back each winter, rather than growing as a tree. Its berries point up the stem, not hang down. Mountain ash has bright-red berries in similar clusters. Colour alone separates them. Deadly nightshade has single black berries on a low plant. The cluster pattern is the easy way to tell them apart.
Part used in this profile: the ripe berries, picked when fully deep-purple. Stems and underripe berries are stripped out at processing, both contain higher concentrations of
cyanogenic glycosides
than the ripe fruit. Dried elderberries from a herbalist supplier are the kitchen norm where foraging isn't practical.
Constituents and actions
The principal constituents of the ripe berry are the
anthocyanins

, the deep-purple pigments. Flavonoids (quercetin, rutin) follow. Then small amounts of phenolic acids, and Vitamin C. Modern open-access research most often links the immune-shifting action to the anthocyanins. Major herbal-medicine reviews cite this constituent set as the basis for the syrup's traditional use.
Western herbal practice names three primary actions for the cooked berry:

Shifts the immune response. This is the basis for the cold-season syrup. The phrasing matters. We say elderberry is "traditionally taken at the first sign of a cold". We do not call it "immune-boosting". Open-access reviews and case-based trials support the traditional use with limited evidence. We describe it at the level the evidence allows.
Diaphoretic

, the warm syrup taken hot through a feverish cold. Old herbals credit it with supporting the body's own sweating response. Modern herbal practice keeps the use for the comfort of a hot sweet syrup, not for any claimed clinical action.

Mild antiviral: open-access lab work studies the constituents, but we keep this term inside the traditional frame. The accurate wording is "traditionally taken to support the body through a cold". A reader who wants the syrup as a clinical antiviral wants more evidence than the herbal library can give. We do not make the claim.
Traditional uses

Western herbal: classic English herbals devote one of their longest entries to the elder, treating bark, leaf, flower, and berry as four separate medicines. The berry, the books note, makes a cooling drink in feverish complaints. The wine is especially valued in colds and rheumatism. The hot syrup through a cold was the standard country medicine across rural Britain in the 18th and 19th centuries.

Folk European: continental tradition uses elderberry in winter syrups. The German Holundersaft (elder cordial) is a household staple. Families press the syrup each autumn and store it through the cold season. The Danish Holunderbeerensuppe (elderberry soup) is the same tradition in soup form, served hot with bread dumplings through colds.

American records: a late 1800s American herbal book lists elderberry as a popular home remedy for colds. It notes the syrup made with sugar, taken hot at the first sign of a cold. The American use closely matched the western European one.
Preparations

Syrup is the kitchen default. Dried elderberries simmered in water (1:10 by weight, 30 minutes covered, strained), then sweetened 1:1 by volume with honey or sugar. The honey version is the traditional household form, gentler on the throat, longer shelf life refrigerated. Dose: 1-2 teaspoons taken at the first sign of a cold, repeated 3-4 times a day for the duration of an acute episode. Course length: 5-7 days at full dose; longer should be a herbalist's call.

Simmered drink: dried berries simmered in water as above, strained, drunk warm without the syrup-stage sweetening. Useful in households where added sugar is not wanted. Same dose pattern as the syrup, in cups rather than spoonfuls. Does not keep. Make the day's batch fresh.

Tincture: dried berries steeped 1:5 with 40% spirit for 4 to 6 weeks. Dose 1 to 3 ml three times a day at the first sign of a cold. This is not our first form for elderberry. The syrup or simmered drink is the form the tradition uses, and the form the evidence base sits with.

What this profile does not include: raw-berry preparations. There is no traditional or modern western herbal preparation of raw uncooked elderberries; eating them raw, even a handful, causes nausea, vomiting, and stomach cramping. The kitchen's elderberry is always cooked, always strained, always free of stems and underripe red berries.
Cautions and contraindications

Cooked before eating: the rule is absolute. Every traditional preparation simmers the berries for at least 30 minutes; the cooking destroys the cyanogenic glycosides that produce the raw-berry symptoms. The publication's preparations all honour this; the home cook should not modify a recipe to skip the simmer.

Identification responsibility: foraging readers should positively ID the plant. The cluster pattern, the tree-form habit (rather than herbaceous perennial), and the pinnate leaves with 5-7 serrated leaflets are the three confirming features. When in doubt, use cultivated dried berries from a herbalist supplier.

Autoimmune conditions: elderberry shifts the immune response. Take care with regular use if you have an autoimmune condition. Talk to a herbalist first. A 5 to 7 day syrup course through a cold is widely tolerated. Daily ongoing use as a tonic is the case where a chat with a herbalist or your GP is the right call.

Drug interactions: no notable interactions at standard doses. Long daily strong syrups can slightly boost the effect of diabetes drugs. The syrup itself carries sugar or honey, which can affect blood sugar. Diabetic readers should account for that. The mild diuretic action can also stack with prescription diuretics. Mention elderberry-syrup use to your prescriber if taking it daily for more than a couple of weeks.
Pregnancy: cooked syrup at standard cold-season doses is widely tolerated. Strong tincture forms are best discussed with a herbalist or midwife first.

Children: standard-strength syrup (1 to 2 teaspoons up to three times a day) is widely tolerated from age 1 upward. It is one of the gentler herbal forms for school-age cold support. Never give honey-sweetened syrup to babies under 12 months. That is the rare botulism risk that applies to all raw honey, not to elderberry alone. For babies, use the sugar-sweetened version at half-strength only with a paediatric herbalist or your GP.

When to skip and see a doctor: any cold lasting more than ten days, a fever above 39°C, laboured breathing, or symptoms that worsen after first getting better. See your GP rather than carry on with the syrup. For children: breathlessness, drowsiness, a drop in how much they drink, or a fever above 39°C in any under-five all need same-day GP review.
